summary:
Market Analysis Update: March 27, 2026

The Canadian bond market enters the final trading days of Q1 2026 characterized by a pronounced "bear flattening" of the yield curve, heavily influenced by a spillover of volatility from the U.S. Treasury market. As of this morning, March 27, yields are attempting to stabilize following yesterday’s hawkish rhetoric from the U.S. Federal Reserve, which signaled that despite cooling inflation, the "terminal rate" may remain higher for longer to combat structural energy costs. Domestically, while February’s 1.8% CPI offered a brief reprieve in late February (sending the 5Y yield to a monthly low of 2.67%), the 5-year GoC yield has since surged to 3.09% as of March 25, closely tracking the 10-year U.S. Treasury's rise. The 2-year/5-year spread has compressed significantly from 35 basis points in mid-February to a narrow 20 basis points, signaling that markets are pricing in aggressive short-term BoC policy retention even as long-term growth expectations soften.

For our institution, this environment has necessitated an immediate pivot in product pricing to protect institutional profitability. After maintaining a "Best 5Y Fixed" rate of 3.94% throughout most of March to defend market share, our lending margin collapsed to a critical low of 0.74% on March 24 as the underlying 5Y bond yield peaked at 3.20%. Consequently, we executed a 5-basis-point hike to 3.99% on March 25, successfully restoring the margin to 0.90%. However, the elevated 2-year yield (hovering near 2.90%) continues to exert upward pressure on our cost of funds. To remain competitive in the deposit space and maintain liquidity, we must likely raise GIC and term deposit rates, which will further compress our Net Interest Margin (NIM) unless the 5-year yield continues to move higher. At this stage, our primary risk remains the high correlation with U.S. volatility; any further hawkishness from the Fed will force additional mortgage rate hikes to prevent our margins from slipping back toward the 0.70% floor.
